significance: Home and burial place of the Baal Shem Tov; foundational birthplace of the chassidic movement.
Birthplace of the Baal Shem Tov.
Seat of the Maggid of Mezeritch in his later years; place of his passing in 1772.
Home of Rabbi Shimon Zundel, an early disciple of the Baal Shem Tov.
Center of the Lithuanian (Misnagdic) tradition; site of fierce early opposition to chassidus. Home of Reb Ze'ev Vilenker before he was drawn to chassidus.
Center of early chassidic activity in Belarus; home of hidden tzaddikim who taught Reb Chaim Eliyahu. Reb Ze'ev Vilenker's home in Vitebsk continued to host chassidic gatherings for generations.
Home of Rabbi Shlomo Raphael.
Birthplace of the Alter Rebbe; site of his early teaching and the first Chabad yeshiva. Setting of the Alter Rebbe's childhood dream involving Rabbi Reuven Baal Shem.
Seat of the Alter Rebbe from 1801 until 1812. Gave the Alter Rebbe the name by which he is most known.
Place of the Alter Rebbe's passing on 24 Tevet 1812 while fleeing Napoleon's advance.
Burial place of the Alter Rebbe.
Center of opposition to the Alter Rebbe among brilliant peers; home of Reb Pinchas Reizes.
Site of opposition to the Alter Rebbe.
Imperial capital where the Alter Rebbe was imprisoned in 1798 and 1800. Reb Mordechai Lepler used his Petersburg home to host the Alter Rebbe after his release.
Home of Rabbi Yekutiel Lepler and Reb Mordechai Lepler.
City where Rabbi Zalman Zezmer served as rabbi.
Town transformed into a Chabad chassidic stronghold through the influence of Rabbi Menachem Zecharia, who won over Rabbi Zerach Meir and the local Torah scholars.
Seat of Chabad from 1813 to 1915, under Rebbes 2 through 5. Home of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva, founded by the Rashab in 1897. Gave the movement its common name 'Lubavitch.'
Town where Reb Hillel served as rabbi, giving him the name by which he is most commonly known.
Major chassidic center; Reb Hillel Paritcher's later location, and home of Rabbi Avraham DovBer of Bobruisk.
Place of Reb Hillel Paritcher's passing in 1864; site of the miraculous fulfillment of his blessing to the tailor.
Home of R' Yitzchak Isaac Epstein, the great Maskil of the Tzemach Tzedek's circle.
Seat of the Kapust branch of Chabad under Rabbi Yehuda Leib, son of the Tzemach Tzedek.
Burial place of the Mitteler Rebbe. Later home of a Chabad branch under one of the Tzemach Tzedek's sons.
Seat of the Avritch branch of Chabad under another of the Tzemach Tzedek's sons.
City associated with Rabbi Shneur Zalman Fradkin, the 'Gaon of Lublin.'
Seat of Chabad from 1915-1924 under the Rashab and early Rayatz. Place of the Rashab's passing in 1920. Setting of major Rayatz farbrengens during the early Soviet years.
City where Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson served as Chief Rabbi and where his sons—including the future Lubavitcher Rebbe—were raised.
Birthplace of the Lubavitcher Rebbe in 1902.
Birthplace of Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka, wife of the Lubavitcher Rebbe.
Place of exile and passing of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson, the Lubavitcher Rebbe's father, in 1944.
Soviet name for St. Petersburg during the years when the Rayatz was based there before his arrest and 1927 expulsion. Place of Rebbetzin Shterna Sarah's passing in 1942.
Center of underground Jewish education established by Reb Simcha Gorodetsky in 1923 under the Rayatz's directives—over 800 students and 45 teachers within three months.
Major center of refuge for Chabad chassidim during WWII; site of R' Zalman Leib Estulin's underground cheder.
Soviet capital; site of underground Chabad activity through the 20th century.
Region of Soviet labor camps and exile where R' Mendel Futerfas, R' Folleh Kahn, and many other chassidim were imprisoned for their religious activities.
Transit point of the 1946 underground 'Exodus from Egypt' organized by R' Mendel Futerfas to smuggle hundreds of chassidic families out of the Soviet Union.
First major stop of the Rayatz after his release from Soviet prison; place where Reb Itche Masmid was martyred in 1941/1942.
Major Chabad center in the interwar period under the Rayatz; site of the Rebbe and Rebbetzin's 1928 wedding. R' Alter Simchovitz served as mashpia of the local Chabad yeshiva.
Town outside Warsaw where the Rayatz established his court before the outbreak of WWII.
Home of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in France, led by R' Nissan Nemenov.
Transit point for many chassidim escaping Europe; the future Rebbe lived here in the late 1930s. Yitzchak Schneersohn (a Tzemach Tzedek descendant) became a successful merchant here.
Transit point for chassidim fleeing the Soviet Union seeking specialist medical care in the West.
Departure port for the Rayatz and the future Rebbe and Rebbetzin as they escaped Europe for the United States in 1940-1941.

name_en: New York (770 Eastern Parkway, Crown Heights)
significance: Final headquarters of Chabad from 1940 onward. The Rayatz arrived in 1940 and established the worldwide Chabad headquarters at 770 Eastern Parkway. The Rebbe led the movement from here from 1951 to 1994.
Site of the 1945 community crisis handled by Rabbi Shlomo Aharon Kazarnovsky.
Place of passing of R' Yisrael Aryeh Leib Schneerson, the Rebbe's younger brother, in 1952.

Holy city; site of Toras Emes yeshiva, the Mea Shearim Chabad community, and the homes of many great chassidim who settled in the Holy Land including R' Chaim Moshe Alperovitz, R' Avraham Chaim Naeh, Rabbi Shashonkin, and R' Asher Sasonkin.
Original location of the Toras Emes yeshiva, established in 1912 by the Rashab who sent an elite group of students including R' Alter Simchovitz to found it.

Major Chabad center in pre-state and modern Israel. Site of the Chabad synagogues on Montefiore and Nachlat Binyamin streets. The Karasik home at Rothschild and Mazeh streets was called 'an institution of the Rebbe.'
Home of a vibrant Chabad community led by Rabbi Meir Blizinsky.
Major Israeli Torah center where Rabbi Yaakov Landau served as Chief Rabbi. Home of R' Chaim Moshe Alperovitz and R' Zalman Leib Estulin in their later years.

Chabad village in Israel established in 1949 by chassidim who escaped the Soviet Union. Home to many of the great mashpiyim of the post-war generation including R' Mendel Futerfas, R' Shlomo Chaim Kesselman, R' Folleh Kahn, R' Berke Chein, and Rabbi Garlick.
Home of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Israel led by Rabbi Shraga Melech Kaplan and Reb Avraham Mayor (Drizin).
Holy city; burial place of Reb Pinye Althaus.
Neighborhood established for Russian-immigrant chassidim. Site of the yeshiva established by Rabbi Motel Kozliner in 1972 at the Rebbe's instruction.
Soviet city where Rabbi Shaul DovBer Zislin served as director and mashpia of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva before moving to the Holy Land.
Soviet city where Reb Zalman Moshe HaYitzchaki lived and farbrenged; major Chabad center in the early Soviet period.
